text.fullText:
As a public health professional, I know how crucial it is for our policies to stress prevention and the precautionary principle. There is enormous evidence that your deregulatory approach will bring avoidable illness, suffering, and death. In addition, the damage to health will result in damage to the economy.

To Administrator Zeldin,

I'm appalled at the EPA plan to undermine Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA). I very strongly oppose the changes that the EPA now proposes.

Under the Clean Water Act, states and tribes have a vital tool to help protect water quality fir their people. Your proposed changes to Section 401 would recklessly limit that scope. Those changes would block regulators from looking beyond the pollution discharged into waterways to evaluate the overall water quality impact of a project.

States and authorized Tribes must be enabled to determine the scope of review needed to protect water quality. They must not be restricted to a shrunken range of federally-protected waters and permitted only to examine direct effects of discharges into those waters.

The broader water-quality effects caused by a project as a whole are what communities rely on the Clean Water Act to analyze and mitigate.

Far beyond the spot where discharge occurs, water quality can be damaged. Limiting the scope of any Section 401 analysis would block crucial reviews of the impacts upstream and downstream of planned projects.

Under the changes that the EPA has proposed, states and Tribes could lose their ability to use Section 401 to protect many smaller streams, wetlands and headwaters. That would leave state and tribal decision-makers unable to block, or put conditions on, federally permitted projects that could damage those waters -- even if those conditions are vital to the safety of local drinking water supplies, recreation, flood control, or fisheries.

Restricting the scope and timeline of analyses would undermine the CWA's goal of ensuring that states, territories, and Tribes are enabled to protect their water resources. It would instead only benefit the corporations advancing project proposals. Taxpayers would wind up burdened by the costs of added cleanup of pollution that couldn't be mitigated because it didn't get analyzed due to the discharge only constraints. Such a restriction would mean approvals for dangerous projects that should not be permitted. Absent proper review, regulators and the public would very sadly fail to learn of harmful impacts and risks that would, under existing policies, be revealed during analyses that we currently benefit from.

The proposed change is unconscionable.
I urge the EPA to instead uphold and strengthen the Clean Water Act to protect drinking water and species habitat in waterways and wetlands, and protect the health of the public.
